This file is the template for the glad's CMake package configuarion file. It is used to generate gladConfig.cmake, which is installed to <prefix>/lib/cmake/glad. Having this file installed (along with the other 'targets' and 'versions' cmake files) allows the package to be used with CMake's newer package configuration system. No more writing 'FindXXX.cmake' files by hand! 
Using the newer package configuration system, glad can be included in a project just by installing it to a prefix and including the following lines of CMake:
```
find_package(glad CONFIG REQUIRED)
...
target_link_libraries(target glad::glad)
# Note: include directories are also imported by the above line, just by using the glad target.
```
(Note that "glad::" is the value namespace variable defined in the CMakeLists.txt file)

It's interesting the understand why this test started failing. Initially, for AAF, I used `self.predict_cumulative_hazard(X).iloc[-1].to_frame()` (no negative sign). This means that higher values would mean higher chances of dieing (as it's a hazard). But this test failed because the concordance was backwards - I would rank individuals who have a predicted small lifetimes as having the highest. So I added the negative sign and everything works. 

The problem is now in the interpretation of `predict` for use outside concordance index metrics. The user will get back negative values, which is kind of odd.

Think about the identity function, we can always use it as the activation. When we use the LeakyReLU activation, the weights should be initialized using the initializer for the deep CNNs. Also, I think a modern definition of LSTM or GRU should be generalized to allow any kinds of inner activation function. We need only to fix the activation of the gate to be the sigmoid function (or even the hard version called the Binary Stochastic Neuron) and we are free to choose the activation for the cell or other things.

```python
from __future__ import absolute_import, division, print_function
```

changes the value returned here.

For example if `len(self._filelist)` were 10 and `self.batch_size` were 6, then in python 2.x one finds
```bash
>>> from math import ceil
>>> int(ceil(10 / 6))
1
>>> from __future__ import division
>>> int(ceil(10 / 6))
2
```

We need this because we moved the batching logic into the worker. When the scheduler sends the worker a bunch of batched tasks, it doesn't know what task the worker will end up running. The worker might start running one of the batched tasks or it could create a new task that the scheduler doesn't know about yet. So it has to inform the scheduler how it's running the batch.

We could add a separate API call for this response, but it would likely have to call `add_task` at least as a first step. I guess it could do that and then mark it running after to keep the `status != RUNNING` invariant if that's important.

Nit: Generally, the assignment should take place outside the try block if you're using the value in the finally block.

    x = foo
    try:
        # ...
    finally:
        do_stuff_with(x)

That's because if it's inside the try block, and the block fails before assigning, the variable will be undefined inside the finally block. This doesn't apply as strongly here because `sys.exc_info()` will never fail, but it still makes sense to assign outside the block.

> ['/v1/*', ['a/b'], {}, '??']

This will not fail to expand, but will fail to validate.

I could make this fail during expansion by checking that the value for a `*` positional arg is a single segment.

> ['/v1/*', ['a', 'b'], {}, '??']

This will not fail to expand, the additional positional arg will just be ignored.

I could make this fail during expansion by checking that there are no leftover positional or named args.

> ['/v1/{name}', [], {'name': 'parent/child'}, '??'],

This will expand, but not validate.

I could make this fail during expansion using the same method as the first case.

> ['/v1/{name=parent/*}', [], {'name': 'doggy/cat'}, '??'],

This will expand, but not validate.

I could make this fail during expansion by doing a validation step for the sub template.

See the merged version for this. The general principle is that the API class (like TabStops) calls the oxml layer for whatever it needs in the XML domain. Most of that is provided by the meta class, so automatically available once you define the CT_x class and its ZeroOrOne's and whatever. However, if something fancier is needed, perhaps to navigate across more than one level in the XML hierarchy, additional methods are added in the oxml layer. So _get_tabs(), if it were needed, would go in CT_Tabs.

It's not needed in this case though, you can see the merged version for how that works out. Basically, the w:tabs element needs to have something in it (at least one w:tab element) or be removed. So we can't add one just to see how many are in it. We have to test for the not present condition and return zero in that case.

I've tested this further. The only scenarios in which I've been able to produce errors upon unsubscribe or auto_renew is upon network disconnects, now that the timeout problem has been addressed. 

Raising an exception on auto_renew seems pointless as there's no calling code to catch the error. Therefore I think it's appropriate to log a warning.

Raising an exception on subscribe does seem right, as the subscribe attempt will have failed and the calling code needs to deal with that.

On reflection, I think a warning is appropriate on unsubscribe for the following reasons:

- It's not quite true to say the unsubscribe attempt has failed (in the way a subscribe attempt would have failed). The subscription has been cancelled and deleted from the list of subscriptions. In that sense, it has been unsubscribed. It's just that the notification of 'UNSUBSCRIBE' to the sonos system failed, most likely because the network or the sonos system was down. It's hard to see what more the calling code could do about that, beyond what has already been done. It seems appropriate to warn about the problem through the logging system, rather than raise an exception.

- Unsubscribe is called automatically at exit / shutdown, where there is again no calling code to catch the exception. It also seems these exceptions at exit aren't reported by python. Query whether that means unsubscribe isn't being fully executed at exit.

My proposal therefore is to raise an exception on a subscribe failure, but to log a warning on an auto_renew or unsubscribe failure.

It's been a long time since I've looked at it, but the first reactive Hadoop ecosystem charms also did some cool stuff, like exchanging Hadoop and Java versions and checking if they matched.

These conversations are also very useful for "upgrading" relationships in a backwards-compatible way. For example, with the postgres charms, the behavior is different for a charm using the "old" protocol vs the "new" protocol.

I couldn't have said it better than @galgalesh.  Relations have always been capable of being more than just static data bags, and the postgres charm is the perfect example of how that sort of thing should work.  Conceivably, the conversation between two charms could go back and forth multiple times.  (The conversation metaphor was originally an attempt to highlight that possibility.)  The main thing limiting this in the past was that every charm had to reimplement that protocol and ensure that they all did it in the same way.  Interface layers were designed to **be** that common specification of how the communication over the relation should occur; that's why I always emphasize that interface layers should contain no implementation and should just define an API for the communication process while encapsulating exactly how that communication takes place "over the wire."

Lets look at the code under test again:
```python
                    for achall, response in zip(self.dv_c, dv_resp):
                        if not self._simple_verify(achall, response):

                                "Self-verify of %s challenge (%s) "
```

This is equivalent to:
```python
                    for achall, response in zip(self.dv_c, dv_resp):
                        retval = self._simple_verify(achall, response)

                                "Self-verify of %s challenge (%s) "
                                "for domain %s failed.",
                                achall.chall.__class__.__name__,
                                achall.domain, achall.uri)
```

In this last bit, the line `if not retval:` is at the core of how the code is supposed to work, but it is not properly tested. It can be replaced with many other lines that are wrong but pass this test, for example `if achall.domain == 'localhost':` or `if hasattr(response, 'simple_verify'):` etc.

a different way to do this might be to bind the fields to their names in `SchemaMeta`.

that way, assuming the field has been bound to its name, then we can look up the name of the field from the field object, and not have to care about the MRO. it would also let us get rid of much of the extra code here around building the extra mapping.

the downside, then, is that this would limit the re-use of field objects. so for example, right now i believe this is okay:

```python
field = fields.Integer()

class FooSchema(Schema):
    foo = field

class BarSchema(Schema):
    bar = field
```

the straightforward way to do this (binding the field object to the assigned name in `SchemaMeta`) would prohibit the above, though it would still allow

```python
field = fields.Integer()

class FooSchema(Schema):
    foo = field

class BarSchema(Schema):
    foo = field
```

> When using a hanging indent the following should be considered; there should be no arguments on the first line and *further indentation should be used* to clearly distinguish itself as a continuation line.

```python
# More indentation included to distinguish this from the rest.
def long_function_name(
        var_one,
        var_two,
        var_three,
```

No, the backend should spawn a thread to do the inference, if it does inference on the CPU, and wait until the event is signaled in this thread. If it does inference on something else then the CPU, it instead should to submit a "wait for event" command to the command queue for the async device, e.g. [cudaStreamWaitEvent](https://www.cs.cmu.edu/afs/cs/academic/class/15668-s11/www/cuda-doc/html/group__CUDART__STREAM_gfe68d207dc965685d92d3f03d77b0876.html), [clEnqueueWaitForEvents](https://www.khronos.org/registry/OpenCL/sdk/1.1/docs/man/xhtml/clEnqueueWaitForEvents.html), or [glWaitSync](https://www.khronos.org/registry/OpenGL-Refpages/es3.0/html/glWaitSync.xhtml).
